<think>

The history of artificial intelligence traces its origins to Alan Turing’s 1950 paper, which introduced the Turing test, and gained formal momentum at the 1956 Dartmouth workshop where John McCarthy coined the term "artificial intelligence." Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, researchers developed foundational technologies like expert systems and natural language interfaces, though progress was hindered by hardware limitations and interrupted by two "AI winters" caused by fluctuating funding. The field ultimately underwent a transformative resurgence in the 2010s, driven by deep learning and decades of

<think>

The history of artificial intelligence traces its origins to Alan Turing’s 1950 paper, which introduced the Turing test, and gained formal momentum at the 1956 Dartmouth workshop where John McCarthy coined the term "artificial intelligence." Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, researchers developed foundational technologies like expert systems and natural language interfaces, though progress was hindered by hardware limitations and interrupted by two "AI winters" caused by fluctuating funding. The field ultimately underwent a transformative resurgence in the 2010s, driven by deep learning and decades of
prompt: The history of artificial intelligence began in earnest with the work of Alan Turing, who in 1950 published the influential paper Computing Machinery and Intelligence, introducing the imitation game now widely known as the Turing test. Following Turings pioneering ideas, the field saw rapid growth during the 1956 Dartmouth workshop organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Nathaniel Rochester, and Claude Shannon. McCarthy coined the term artificial intelligence for the workshop. Through the 1960s and 1970s, researchers developed expert systems, theorem provers, and natural language interfaces, though hardware limitations of the era constrained the scale at which these systems could operate. Funding cycles produced two notable AI winters before deep learning, building on three decades of neural network research, transformed the field starting in the 2010s. The history of artificial intelligence began in earnest with the work of Alan Turing, who in 1950 published the influential paper Computing Machinery and Intelligence, introducing the imitation game now widely known as the Turing test. Following Turings pioneering ideas, the field saw rapid growth during the 1956 Dartmouth workshop organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Nathaniel Rochester, and Claude Shannon. McCarthy coined the term artificial intelligence for the workshop. Through the 1960s and 1970s, researchers developed expert systems, theorem provers, and natural language interfaces, though hardware limitations of the era constrained the scale at which these systems could operate. Funding cycles produced two notable AI winters before deep learning, building on three decades of neural network research, transformed the field starting in the 2010s.
